questions.question: In computer architecture, Flynn's Taxonomy classifies computer systems based on the number of instruction streams and data streams. Which category describes a single processor executing a single instruction stream on multiple data streams simultaneously?
options: SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)
SISD (Single Instruction, Single Data)
MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data)
MISD (Multiple Instruction, Single Data)
SPMD (Single Program, Multiple Data)

questions.question: In the digital IC design flow, what is the primary objective of Static Timing Analysis (STA)?
options: To verify the functional correctness of the design by simulating its behavior with test vectors.
To ensure all logical paths in the circuit meet their timing constraints without requiring test vectors.
To extract parasitic resistances and capacitances from the layout for post-layout simulation.
To optimize power consumption by identifying and gating unused clock signals.
To synthesize the Register-Transfer Level (RTL) design into a gate-level netlist.

questions.question: During the initial floorplanning phase of an ASIC design, what is a crucial consideration for macro placement and block partitioning, especially with respect to global timing closure?
options: Minimizing the total number of standard cells to reduce die area.
Ensuring even distribution of power rails to prevent localized electromigration issues.
Reducing the maximum fan-out of high-drive cells in peripheral logic.
Optimizing the routing channels to minimize wirelength and critical path delays between major functional blocks.
Characterizing the standard cell library for power, timing, and area metrics.

questions.question: In Verilog HDL, what is the fundamental difference in behavior between a blocking assignment (`=`) and a non-blocking assignment (`<=`) when used in a `always @(posedge clk)` block?
options: Blocking assignments are primarily used for combinational logic, while non-blocking assignments are exclusively for sequential logic.
Blocking assignments evaluate and update variables immediately within the current procedural block, whereas non-blocking assignments schedule updates to occur at the end of the current time step.
Non-blocking assignments simulate faster than blocking assignments due to parallel execution.
Blocking assignments inherently prevent race conditions, while non-blocking assignments are more prone to them in sequential logic.
There is no functional difference between them; they are merely stylistic alternatives depending on design intent.

questions.question: As semiconductor manufacturing processes scale down to advanced nodes (e.g., 7nm, 5nm), transistor characteristics and interconnect properties exhibit increasing variability across a die and wafer. What is a common design technique employed to mitigate the impact of such 'process variations' on circuit timing and performance?
options: Implementing a highly synchronous design style with a single global clock domain and no clock gating.
Utilizing only blocking assignments (`=`) in all Register-Transfer Level (RTL) code to ensure deterministic behavior.
Employing guard banding, designing for worst-case corners, or implementing adaptive body biasing (ABB).
Reducing the number of metal layers used in the physical layout to simplify manufacturing.
Increasing the supply voltage (VDD) indiscriminately across the entire chip to overcome threshold voltage variations.
